drills.title: Build a search tokenizer
drills.prompt: Write a tokenizer that lowercases, splits on non-alphanumerics, removes a small stop-word set, and applies simple suffix stemming. Use the exact same function for indexing and querying.
drills.expectedOutput: tokenize('The Quick Brown Foxes!') → ['quick','brown','fox']
hints: The analyzer must be symmetric across index and query time.
Keep stop words minimal — they hurt short queries.
drills.solutionNotes: Stemming should be conservative; aggressive stemming makes distinct words collide.

drills.title: Build an inverted index
drills.prompt: From a list of documents, build a term → sorted posting list map. Support a boolean AND query by intersecting posting lists. Track document frequency per term.
drills.expectedOutput: Query 'quick fox' returns docs containing both terms, found by list intersection.
hints: Keep posting lists sorted by doc id so intersection is linear.
Store document frequency now — ranking needs it later.
drills.solutionNotes: Separate the dictionary (term → metadata) from the postings storage.

drills.title: Calculate a TF-IDF score by hand
drills.prompt: Given term counts per document and document frequencies, compute the TF-IDF weight of a term in a document. Use dampened TF (1 + log tf) and IDF = log(N / df).
drills.expectedOutput: A single numeric weight, higher for frequent-here-but-rare-overall terms.
hints: IDF rewards rarity across the corpus.
drills.solutionNotes: TF-IDF ignores document length — that is exactly what BM25 fixes.

drills.title: Calculate a BM25 score by hand
drills.prompt: Given tf, df, N, document length, average document length, k1=1.2 and b=0.75, compute the BM25 score of a query term for a document.
drills.expectedOutput: A numeric score; verify TF saturation by checking the 10th occurrence adds little.
hints: b controls length normalization; k1 controls TF saturation.
drills.solutionNotes: Try b=0 (no length norm) and b=1 (full) to feel the effect.

drills.title: Implement a BM25 ranker
drills.prompt: On top of your inverted index, implement BM25 scoring and return the top-k documents for a multi-term query.
drills.expectedOutput: Ranked top-k results for a query, scored by summed per-term BM25.
hints: Sum per-term BM25 contributions for multi-term queries.
Use a k-sized heap for the top-k.
drills.solutionNotes: This ranker is the lexical leg of hybrid search.

drills.title: Compute precision and recall by hand
drills.prompt: Given retrieved doc ids [1,4,2,9] and relevant ids {2,4,7}, compute precision@4 and recall@4.
drills.expectedOutput: precision@4 = 0.5 (2 relevant in 4), recall@4 = 2/3.
hints: Recall = relevant retrieved / total relevant in corpus.
drills.solutionNotes: MRR and nDCG add rank position — precision/recall are set-based.

testCases.run: const { p, r } = precisionRecall([1,4,2,9], [2,4,7]); console.log(p.toFixed(2), r.toFixed(2));

drills.title: Build a search eval harness
drills.prompt: Create a labelled query set (query → relevant doc ids) and a harness that scores any retriever with recall@k, MRR, and nDCG.
drills.expectedOutput: A single command that prints the three metrics for a retriever.
hints: Freeze the query set before tuning.
Score retrievers through one common interface.
drills.solutionNotes: Without this harness, every ranking change is a guess.

drills.title: Implement reciprocal rank fusion
drills.prompt: Given two ranked result lists (BM25 and vector), fuse them with RRF: score(d) = Σ 1/(k + rank_i(d)), k≈60.
drills.expectedOutput: A single fused ranking that uses rank, not raw scores.
hints: RRF uses ranks, sidestepping incomparable score scales.
A document missing from a list simply contributes nothing from it.
drills.solutionNotes: Verify the fused list beats both inputs on your eval set.

drills.title: Embed a corpus and search it
drills.prompt: Embed a small corpus with an embedding model, embed a query, and return the nearest documents by cosine similarity.
drills.expectedOutput: Semantically relevant documents for a query that shares no exact keywords.
hints: Use the same model for corpus and query.
drills.solutionNotes: This is the vector leg of hybrid search.

testCases.run:
const corpus = [{ id: 'a', vec: [1, 0] }, { id: 'b', vec: [0.9, 0.1] }, { id: 'c', vec: [0, 1] }];
console.log(JSON.stringify(embedSearch(corpus, [1, 0], 2)));

drills.title: Implement similarity metrics
drills.prompt: Implement cosine similarity, dot product, and L2 distance. Show that on normalized vectors, cosine and dot product produce the same ranking.
drills.expectedOutput: Three functions; a demonstration that normalized cosine == dot ranking.
drills.solutionNotes: The metric must match what the embedding model was trained for.

drills.title: Top-k with a bounded heap
drills.prompt: Given a stream of (id, score) pairs, return the top-k by score using a size-k min-heap — without sorting everything.
drills.expectedOutput: Correct top-k in O(n log k) time and O(k) space.
hints: Keep a min-heap of size k; the root is the weakest kept item.
Replace the root when a better score arrives.
drills.solutionNotes: This selection pattern is the core of every nearest-neighbour search.

drills.title: Build a brute-force vector index
drills.prompt: Build an index that stores vectors and answers top-k queries by scanning every vector. Benchmark latency as the vector count grows.
drills.expectedOutput: Exact top-k results; a latency-vs-n table.
hints: Batch the scan as a matrix multiply.
drills.solutionNotes: Every ANN index is measured against this for recall.

drills.title: Explain HNSW search on paper
drills.prompt: Draw a 3-layer HNSW graph and trace a query: which layer it enters, how it greedily hops, when it descends, and where efSearch widens the search.
drills.expectedOutput: A diagram + walkthrough showing O(log n) traversal instead of O(n) scan.
hints: Upper layers = long hops; lower layers = fine search.
drills.solutionNotes: HNSW is approximate — there is no exactness guarantee.

drills.title: Add metadata filtering to vector search
drills.prompt: Extend your brute-force index to support 'nearest WHERE field = value'. Implement both pre-filtering and post-filtering and compare results and timing.
drills.expectedOutput: Filtered top-k that always returns k items when k qualify.
hints: Post-filtering an ANN result can return fewer than k.
Pre-filtering is exact but scans the filtered subset.
drills.solutionNotes: Pre vs post filtering is a core vector DB design decision.

drills.title: Build a minimal RAG pipeline
drills.prompt: Chunk a small document set, embed and index it, retrieve for a query, pack context, and generate a grounded answer.
drills.expectedOutput: Answers grounded in retrieved chunks, with sources.
hints: Retrieval quality is the ceiling — debug retrieval first.
drills.solutionNotes: Most RAG failures are retrieval failures, not generation failures.

drills.title: Build a structure-aware chunker
drills.prompt: Write a chunker that splits on document structure (headings/paragraphs) with a target size and overlap, instead of fixed character cuts.
drills.expectedOutput: Chunks that respect structure and overlap at boundaries.
hints: Different document types may need different strategies.
drills.solutionNotes: Chunk size is a retrieval-quality knob — tune it against evals.

drills.title: Schema-constrained extraction
drills.prompt: Define a JSON schema and use structured-output mode to extract typed fields from unstructured text. Validate the result and reject on failure.
drills.expectedOutput: A validated typed object, or an explicit rejection.
hints: Use schema/JSON mode, not regex on prose.
drills.solutionNotes: Keep schemas shallow enough for the model to fill reliably.

testCases.run:
const r = extractInvoice('Acme Corp billed $42.50 on 2024-06-01');
console.log(r.ok, r.data.amount);

drills.title: Build a tool-calling loop
drills.prompt: Give an LLM two tools (e.g. search and calculator). Run a loop: model emits a tool call, you execute it, feed the result back, repeat until done.
drills.expectedOutput: A task solved across multiple tool calls.
drills.solutionNotes: Add a step limit so the loop cannot run away.

drills.title: Build an LLM eval
drills.prompt: Create a dataset of inputs with expected behavior and a grader (deterministic or LLM-as-judge). Run it as one command and score a prompt.
drills.expectedOutput: A pass-rate score that changes when you change the prompt.
hints: Cover real failure modes, not just happy paths.
Validate an LLM judge against human labels.
drills.solutionNotes: Evals are unit tests for non-deterministic systems.

drills.title: Implement a token-bucket rate limiter
drills.prompt: Implement a token bucket: tokens refill at a fixed rate, each request spends one, an empty bucket returns 429 with Retry-After.
drills.expectedOutput: Allows bursts up to bucket size, then throttles to the refill rate.
hints: Compute refill lazily from elapsed time.
drills.solutionNotes: For a cluster, the bucket state must be shared (e.g. Redis).

testCases.run:
const b = TokenBucket(2, 1);
console.log(b.allow().ok, b.allow().ok, b.allow().ok);

drills.title: Implement idempotency keys
drills.prompt: Add idempotency-key handling to a mutating endpoint: first request executes and stores the result; duplicates replay the stored result.
drills.expectedOutput: Two identical requests with the same key cause one effect.
hints: Store key → result with an expiry.
Guard against two concurrent requests with the same key.
drills.solutionNotes: Idempotency makes retries safe — pair it with retry logic.

drills.title: Implement backoff with jitter
drills.prompt: Implement exponential backoff with full jitter for a retry loop, and route a message to a DLQ after N failed attempts.
drills.expectedOutput: Retry delays that grow exponentially and are randomized; poison messages land in a DLQ.
hints: Jitter de-syncs many clients retrying at once.
drills.solutionNotes: Without jitter, all clients retry in lockstep — a thundering herd.

drills.title: Build a memtable + SSTable flush
drills.prompt: Implement an in-memory memtable that flushes to an immutable sorted file (SSTable). Support reads that merge memtable and SSTables; deletes as tombstones.
drills.expectedOutput: Writes go to the memtable; reads see the latest value across files.
hints: A read may need to check several files newest-first.
drills.solutionNotes: Add bloom filters so reads skip files that lack the key.

drills.title: Build a write-ahead log
drills.prompt: Implement an append-only WAL: append + fsync each change before acknowledging it, and replay the log on startup to recover state.
drills.expectedOutput: State survives a simulated crash by replaying the log.
hints: fsync before you acknowledge the write.
Checkpoint so the log does not grow forever.
drills.solutionNotes: A write() syscall does not mean the data is durable — fsync does.

drills.title: Two Sum with a hash map
drills.prompt: Given an array of integers and a target, return the indices of the two numbers that add to the target. Solve it in a single pass.
hints: For each number, what value would complete the pair?
Store value -> index as you scan; check for the complement before inserting.
drills.solutionNotes: One pass, O(n) time, O(n) space. The map turns the inner scan into an O(1) lookup.

drills.title: Valid palindrome with two pointers
drills.prompt: Given a string, decide if it reads the same forwards and backwards, ignoring case and non-alphanumeric characters. Use two pointers, no extra string.
drills.expectedOutput: isPalindrome('A man, a plan, a canal: Panama') -> true
hints: Start one pointer at each end, move inward.
Skip non-alphanumeric characters without allocating a cleaned copy.
drills.solutionNotes: Converging pointers give O(n) time and O(1) space.

testCases.run: console.log(isPalindrome('A man, a plan, a canal: Panama'));

drills.title: Longest substring without repeats
drills.prompt: Find the length of the longest substring with no repeating characters using a variable-size sliding window.
drills.expectedOutput: lengthOfLongestSubstring('abcabcbb') -> 3
hints: Expand the right edge; on a duplicate, shrink the left edge until the window is valid again.
Track the last index of each character.
drills.solutionNotes: Each character enters and leaves the window once — O(n).

drills.title: Search in a rotated sorted array
drills.prompt: A sorted array was rotated at an unknown pivot. Find a target's index in O(log n).
hints: At each step one half is still sorted — identify which.
Decide whether the target lies in the sorted half, then recurse there.
drills.solutionNotes: Standard binary search with an extra check for which half is ordered.

drills.title: Binary tree level-order traversal
drills.prompt: Return the values of a binary tree grouped by level, top to bottom.
hints: Levels mean BFS — use a queue.
Process the queue one level at a time by capturing its size before the loop.
drills.solutionNotes: BFS with a per-level size snapshot; O(n).

testCases.run:
const root = { v: 3, l: { v: 9 }, r: { v: 20, l: { v: 15 }, r: { v: 7 } } };
console.log(JSON.stringify(levelOrder(root)));

drills.title: Number of islands
drills.prompt: Given a grid of '1' (land) and '0' (water), count the connected land masses (4-directionally).
drills.expectedOutput: A single integer — the island count.
hints: Each unvisited '1' starts a new island; flood-fill it with DFS or BFS.
Mark cells visited as you go to avoid recounting.
drills.solutionNotes: Grid-as-graph: every cell is a node, neighbours are edges. O(rows*cols).

drills.title: House robber (1D DP)
drills.prompt: Given values along a line, pick a non-adjacent subset with maximum sum.
hints: Define dp[i] = best sum using houses 0..i.
drills.solutionNotes: Classic 1D DP; can be reduced to two rolling variables, O(1) space.

drills.title: Kth largest element with a heap
drills.prompt: Return the k-th largest element of an unsorted array using a heap, without fully sorting.
drills.expectedOutput: findKthLargest([3,2,1,5,6,4], 2) -> 5
hints: Keep a min-heap of size k; the root is the answer.
If the heap exceeds k, pop the smallest.
drills.solutionNotes: O(n log k) time, O(k) space — beats sorting when k is small.

drills.title: Design a parking lot
drills.prompt: Model a parking lot with vehicle classes (Car/Bike/Truck), spot sizes, a Lot/Level/Spot hierarchy, and entry/exit. Define the 4-6 classes, their fields, and the park()/leave() interactions.
drills.expectedOutput: Class diagram + the park() flow showing how spot allocation handles size mismatch.
hints: Composition over inheritance for vehicle ↔ spot fit.
Where does pricing live — Spot, Level, or Lot?
drills.solutionNotes: Avoid one mega-class. Spot allocation is the real decision point.

drills.title: Design a vending machine
drills.prompt: Draw the state machine for a vending machine: Idle → Coin → Selecting → Dispense → Idle, plus refund / out-of-stock branches. List every transition trigger.
drills.expectedOutput: A state diagram with 5-7 states, transitions labeled by input event.
hints: What does pressing a button do in each state?
drills.solutionNotes: States hold behavior; transitions hold guards. Keep illegal transitions impossible.

drills.title: Pluggable pricing strategies
drills.prompt: Design a checkout system where pricing strategy is swappable: flat, percentage-off, BOGO, member-tier. Each strategy is a class with the same price(cart) signature.
drills.expectedOutput: Interface + 4 concrete strategies + how the cart picks one at runtime.
hints: Inject the strategy; do not let cart code know its type.
drills.solutionNotes: Strategy pattern shines when an algorithm varies independently of its caller.

drills.title: In-process pub/sub bus
drills.prompt: Build an in-memory event bus: publishers fire events, subscribers register handlers by topic. Decide sync vs async delivery, error isolation, and unsubscribe semantics.
drills.expectedOutput: Bus interface (publish, subscribe, unsubscribe) + the subscriber registry data structure.
hints: What happens if one subscriber throws?
Keep the bus simple — no ordering guarantee unless asked.
drills.solutionNotes: Observer decouples emitter and listener; bus is the registry between them.

drills.title: Notification factory
drills.prompt: Design a Notification factory that returns Email / SMS / Push notifiers from a single create(type, payload) call. Show where channel-specific config lives.
drills.expectedOutput: Factory + 3 concrete notifiers + the registration mechanism for adding a 4th.
hints: Map of type → constructor beats a switch.
Keep the factory dumb; let notifiers carry their own config.
drills.solutionNotes: Factories pay off when you add the 4th type without touching callers.

drills.title: Coffee-shop order decorator
drills.prompt: Model a coffee order with milk / sugar / whip add-ons via the decorator pattern. cost() and description() should compose automatically.
drills.expectedOutput: Base Beverage + 3 decorators, plus a sample order chain and its computed cost.
hints: Each decorator wraps a Beverage and is itself a Beverage.
Order of wrapping matters for description, not cost.
drills.solutionNotes: Decorator > deep inheritance trees when feature combinations explode.

drills.title: Concurrent bank transfers
drills.prompt: Design transfer(from, to, amount) that is safe under concurrent calls. Address deadlock from circular locking, isolation, and what happens if one side fails mid-write.
drills.expectedOutput: Locking order rule + pseudocode for the transfer + how you would test for deadlock.
hints: Always acquire locks in a globally consistent order (e.g. by account id).
Use a single transaction; rollback on failure.
drills.solutionNotes: Concurrency bugs are not about more locks — they are about a discipline for which locks and in what order.

drills.title: Undo / redo stack
drills.prompt: Design undo/redo for a text editor using the Command pattern. Each user action becomes a Command with execute/undo. Decide how the redo stack interacts with new edits.
drills.expectedOutput: Command interface + 2-3 concrete commands + the two stacks and their invariants.
hints: New action after undo should clear the redo stack.
Group small typing actions into one Command for usability.
drills.solutionNotes: Each command must store enough state to perfectly invert itself.

drills.title: Hotel booking system
drills.prompt: Design a hotel-room booking system: search availability for a date range, hold a room during checkout, finalize the reservation. Address concurrent bookers fighting for the last room.
drills.expectedOutput: Inventory model + hold/confirm flow + the consistency strategy (optimistic vs pessimistic).
hints: A hold is itself a write — model it.
drills.solutionNotes: Overbooking is a business decision, not just a race condition.

drills.title: L7 load balancer
drills.prompt: Design an L7 load balancer in front of N application servers. Pick a routing algorithm (round-robin, least-connections, hashing), explain health checks, and how you drain a node.
drills.expectedOutput: Routing decision tree + health-check loop + a graceful-drain sequence.
hints: Sticky sessions cost you flexibility — only enable when needed.
Health checks should mimic real traffic, not just /ping.
drills.solutionNotes: The hard part is not picking an algorithm — it is failing safely when a backend goes bad.

drills.title: Distributed rate limiter
drills.prompt: Design a rate limiter for an API: 1000 req/min per user, deployed across 10 app servers. Compare token bucket vs sliding window, and where the counter lives.
drills.expectedOutput: Algorithm choice + storage backend (Redis?) + how you handle a Redis outage.
hints: Per-instance counters undercount; central counters add latency.
drills.solutionNotes: Pick the cheapest approximation that does not violate the contract you advertise.

drills.title: Realtime chat system
drills.prompt: Design a Slack-like chat: 1:1 + channels, presence, delivery + read receipts, offline message queue. Pick the wire protocol and how messages fan out.
drills.expectedOutput: Connection layer + message storage + the fan-out strategy for a 10k-member channel.
hints: WebSocket connections are stateful — that constrains your load balancer.
Fan-out on write vs on read changes everything downstream.
drills.solutionNotes: Hot channels (>10k members) often need a different fan-out path than the long tail.

drills.title: News feed (Twitter-style)
drills.prompt: Design a home timeline feed: who you follow → posts you see, ranked recency-first. Compare fan-out-on-write vs fan-out-on-read. Address celebrity-follower asymmetry.
drills.expectedOutput: Hybrid fan-out strategy + storage layout + a back-of-envelope read/write QPS estimate.
hints: A feed cache that is 80% pre-computed is usually enough.
drills.solutionNotes: Hybrid is the norm: precompute for normal users, pull for celebrities at read time.

drills.title: Video streaming (Netflix-style)
drills.prompt: Design a video-on-demand service: catalog browse, adaptive bitrate streaming, CDN distribution. Walk through what happens from play-button press to first frame on screen.
drills.expectedOutput: CDN + manifest (HLS/DASH) flow + the bitrate-adaptation loop.
hints: Origin servers should never serve video to end users.
Pre-warm the CDN for predictable launches.
drills.solutionNotes: The 99th-percentile player is on bad wifi — design for that, not the demo.

drills.title: Ride-sharing dispatch
drills.prompt: Design driver-rider matching: rider requests, dispatch finds the nearest available driver, ETA + price quoted, ride lifecycle. Address geo-indexing and surge.
drills.expectedOutput: Geo index choice (geohash / S2 / quadtree) + the dispatch decision loop.
hints: Nearest is rarely best — factor ETA, not just distance.
Driver state updates are write-heavy; pick a store that handles that.
drills.solutionNotes: Match quality (not match speed) is the business metric — optimize for it explicitly.

drills.title: Collaborative doc editor
drills.prompt: Design a Google-Docs-style collaborative editor for 2-50 concurrent editors. Pick a conflict resolution model (OT vs CRDT) and explain the sync protocol.
drills.expectedOutput: Conflict model + a worked example of two concurrent edits being merged.
hints: CRDTs are easier to reason about; OT is more compact on the wire.
Cursor presence is a separate channel from edits.
drills.solutionNotes: Pick the model that matches your latency budget — CRDTs tolerate offline, OT does not.

drills.title: Search platform end-to-end
drills.prompt: Design a product search platform: ingestion → analyzer → inverted index → query parser → ranking → result page. Identify where you would add a learned ranker.
drills.expectedOutput: Pipeline diagram + the index update strategy (full rebuild vs near-real-time).
hints: Recall first, precision second — bad recall is unrecoverable downstream.
Two-stage ranking: cheap recall, expensive rerank.
drills.solutionNotes: Search quality lives in the analyzer + the ranker — not the index data structure.

drills.title: Production RAG pipeline
drills.prompt: Design a RAG system: doc ingestion → chunking → embedding → vector index, plus query-time retrieval → rerank → LLM. Identify the 3 most likely quality regressions.
drills.expectedOutput: Pipeline + the evals you would run on every change.
hints: Chunking dominates retrieval quality more than embeddings do.
Eval the retriever and the generator separately.
drills.solutionNotes: Most RAG bugs are upstream of the LLM — fix retrieval before tuning prompts.

drills.title: Consistent-hash key router
drills.prompt: Design a key router for a sharded cache using consistent hashing with virtual nodes. Show how add/remove of a node redistributes keys.
drills.expectedOutput: Ring diagram + the algorithm for routing a key + the % of keys that move when N→N+1 nodes.
hints: Without vnodes the distribution is lumpy.
Hash the node id with k different seeds for vnodes.
drills.solutionNotes: The whole point is that only ~1/N of keys move on a node change.

drills.title: Leader election walkthrough
drills.prompt: Walk through Raft leader election: timeouts, candidacy, vote granting, split votes. Trace what happens when the current leader is partitioned from a majority.
drills.expectedOutput: A 3-node trace showing one election cycle, including a split-vote retry.
hints: Randomized election timeouts break split-vote loops.
Majority quorum, not consensus, is what guarantees safety.
drills.solutionNotes: Safety (no two leaders) is non-negotiable; liveness can briefly stall during partitions.

drills.title: Realtime game server
drills.prompt: Design the server for a 1v1 realtime game (chess clock, but with sub-100ms moves). Cover tick rate, authoritative state, and cheat resistance.
drills.expectedOutput: Tick loop + the input → state-update → broadcast cycle + one cheat mitigation.
drills.solutionNotes: The server is the source of truth; clients exist to make latency tolerable.

drills.title: STAR: leadership story
drills.prompt: Write a 4-paragraph STAR (Situation / Task / Action / Result) about a time you led without authority. Quantify the result.
drills.expectedOutput: A 250-350 word story with one concrete metric in the Result.
hints: Leadership without authority = persuasion + alignment, not org charts.
Cut adjectives; keep verbs and numbers.
drills.solutionNotes: The Action section is where you spend 60% of the words.

drills.title: STAR: peer conflict
drills.prompt: Write a STAR about resolving a disagreement with a peer. Show the other person’s reasoning fairly; describe how you de-escalated.
drills.expectedOutput: A 250-350 word story where the other person is not the villain.
hints: End with what you would do differently.
drills.solutionNotes: Hiring bars reject the "I was right, they were wrong" arc.

drills.title: STAR: tough decision
drills.prompt: Write a STAR about a hard call you made with incomplete information. Show your reasoning and what you tracked to know if you were wrong.
drills.expectedOutput: A story + a single sentence: how would you have known sooner if this was wrong?
hints: Frame it as a bet, not a certainty.
Reversibility matters — was this a one-way door?
drills.solutionNotes: Decision quality > outcome quality. Judge the call by what you knew at the time.

drills.title: STAR: cross-team collab
drills.prompt: Write a STAR about a project that needed another team’s buy-in. Show how you got it without escalation.
drills.expectedOutput: A 250-350 word story emphasizing the alignment work, not the technical work.
hints: Make the other team’s win visible up front.
drills.solutionNotes: Most cross-team friction is unclear ownership — name it explicitly.

drills.title: STAR: a real failure
drills.prompt: Write a STAR about a project that failed. Be specific about what you got wrong. End with what concretely changed in your behavior since.
drills.expectedOutput: A 250-350 word story where the failure is non-trivial and your role in it is named.
hints: "It taught me a lot" is not a lesson.
Pick a failure you actually moved past, not a fresh wound.
drills.solutionNotes: A safe failure (e.g. "I worked too hard") signals you have not learned anything.

drills.title: STAR: explaining to a non-expert
drills.prompt: Write a STAR about explaining a technical concept to a non-technical stakeholder so they could make a decision. Show the analogy or framing you chose.
drills.expectedOutput: A 250-350 word story including the actual analogy you used.
hints: One core analogy beats five small ones.
Audit: what does the stakeholder need to decide?
drills.solutionNotes: Good communication makes the listener’s next decision easier, not yours.

drills.title: STAR: ruthless prioritization
drills.prompt: Write a STAR about a quarter where you had 2x the work you could deliver. Show the cut list and how you got buy-in on it.
drills.expectedOutput: A 250-350 word story including the explicit list of what you did NOT do.
hints: Saying no to a good thing is the actual skill.
A written cut list is a forcing function for buy-in.
drills.solutionNotes: Prioritization stories should make the listener uncomfortable about what got dropped.

drills.title: STAR: an unconventional idea
drills.prompt: Write a STAR about a solution you proposed that was not the obvious one. Show why the obvious one was insufficient.
drills.expectedOutput: A 250-350 word story where the alternative options are credibly evaluated, not strawmanned.
hints: Anchor on a constraint nobody else was taking seriously.
Show your cost-benefit, not just your conclusion.
drills.solutionNotes: Innovation theater is when the "creative" answer ignores known tradeoffs.

drills.title: STAR: acting on customer feedback
drills.prompt: Write a STAR about a time you changed a roadmap based on a single customer signal. Address how you knew it would generalize.
drills.expectedOutput: A 250-350 word story including the disqualifying-or-confirming evidence you collected.
hints: n=1 is a hypothesis, not a conclusion.
Talk to the customer, then look at the data.
drills.solutionNotes: Customer obsession is not saying yes — it is taking their problem seriously, even when you say no.

drills.title: STAR: end-to-end ownership
drills.prompt: Write a STAR about a problem that was not your job but you owned anyway. Show how you escalated when you needed help.
drills.expectedOutput: A 250-350 word story where you stayed accountable even when the work was hard.
hints: Ownership is about closing the loop, not heroics.
Escalation is part of ownership, not a failure of it.
drills.solutionNotes: The opposite of ownership is "that is not my team" — name where you could have said that and did not.

drills.title: Draft a product positioning statement
drills.prompt: Write a 2-sentence positioning statement for a product you use, using the form: "For [target] who [need], [product] is the [category] that [unique benefit]. Unlike [competitor], it [differentiator]." Then write a worse version and explain why yours is sharper.
drills.expectedOutput: Two positioning statements (good + worse) + a one-line critique of the worse one.
drills.solutionNotes: A statement that fits 3 competitors is not positioning.

drills.title: Critique a real landing page
drills.prompt: Pick a real landing page (not your own). Score it against: clear hero (3s rule), specific audience, single primary CTA, social proof, friction-free signup. Write a rewrite for the hero section.
drills.expectedOutput: A scoring rubric (1-5 per criterion) + a rewritten hero (headline + sub + CTA).
hints: Read the headline; close your eyes; can you say what the product does?
Cut adjectives. Replace with nouns and verbs.
drills.solutionNotes: A landing page does one job: get the right visitor to take one action.

drills.title: On-page SEO audit
drills.prompt: Pick a page on a real product site. Audit: title tag, meta description, H1, internal links, mobile usability, Core Web Vitals (LCP target <2.5s). List 3 fixes ranked by impact.
drills.expectedOutput: A short audit table + 3 prioritized fixes with a rationale.
hints: Title tag is the single biggest on-page lever.
LCP is usually one bad image away from green.
drills.solutionNotes: On-page SEO is hygiene; content + links are the actual fight.

drills.title: Define a north-star metric
drills.prompt: Pick a product. Define its north-star metric (one number that captures customer value), 2-3 input metrics that drive it, and a guardrail metric you must not regress.
drills.expectedOutput: A tree: north-star → inputs → guardrail, each with a one-sentence rationale.
hints: North-star is not revenue. It is the value the user gets.
A good guardrail is something you would never trade away.
drills.solutionNotes: The test: can a PM make a tradeoff call from this tree alone?

drills.title: Compute a dot product and angle
drills.prompt: Given u = [3, 4] and v = [1, 2], compute u·v, ||u||, ||v||, and cos θ between them. State whether the vectors are orthogonal.
hints: Dot product is sum of element-wise products.
drills.solutionNotes: The angle formula cos θ = (u·v)/(||u|| ||v||) is the bridge between algebra and geometry.

testCases.run:
const u = [3,4], v = [1,2];
console.log(dot(u,v), norm(u).toFixed(0), cosAngle(u,v).toFixed(2), dot(u,v) === 0 ? 'orth' : 'not');

drills.title: Multiply 2×2 matrices by hand
drills.prompt: Compute AB where A = [[1, 2], [0, 1]] and B = [[1, 0], [3, 1]]. Then apply A to vector x = [1, 1] and B to the result — does AB·x equal A·(B·x)?
drills.expectedOutput: AB = [[7, 2], [3, 1]]; AB·x = [9, 4] = A·(B·x). Matrix multiplication is composition.
hints: Order matters: AB ≠ BA in general.
drills.solutionNotes: The columns of AB are A applied to the columns of B — that's the transformation picture.

drills.title: Interpret an eigenvalue
drills.prompt: Matrix A = [[2, 1], [1, 2]] has eigenvector v = [1, 1] with eigenvalue λ = 3. Explain in one sentence what Av looks like geometrically. What is the other eigenvalue of this symmetric matrix?
drills.expectedOutput: Av = 3v — v is only stretched by 3, not rotated. The other eigenvalue is 1 (eigenvector [1, -1]).
hints: Eigenvectors are fixed directions under the map.
drills.solutionNotes: Symmetric matrices have real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors — PCA relies on this.

testCases.setup:
function applyA(x) { return [2*x[0]+x[1], x[0]+2*x[1]]; }
function eigenvalue2x2Symmetric(a, b, c) { return [(a+c+Math.sqrt((a-c)**2+4*b*b))/2, (a+c-Math.sqrt((a-c)**2+4*b*b))/2]; }

drills.title: Apply Bayes' rule
drills.prompt: A test has 99% sensitivity and 95% specificity. Disease prevalence is 1%. A patient tests positive. Using Bayes' rule, compute P(disease | positive) to 2 decimal places.
drills.expectedOutput: ≈ 0.17 (17%). Most positives are false alarms when the base rate is low.
drills.solutionNotes: Low base rates make naive test interpretation dangerous — always condition on prevalence.

drills.title: Expectation and variance of a discrete RV
drills.prompt: X takes values {0, 1, 2} with probabilities {0.2, 0.5, 0.3}. Compute E[X] and Var(X) = E[X²] − (E[X])².
hints: Compute E[X²] before subtracting the squared mean.
drills.solutionNotes: Variance measures spread around the mean, not around zero.

drills.title: Summarize a small dataset
drills.prompt: Latency samples (ms): 12, 14, 15, 16, 200. Compute mean, median, and sample standard deviation. Which summary is misleading for SLA reporting and why?
drills.expectedOutput: Mean ≈ 51.4 (pulled by outlier), median = 15 (robust), std dev is large. Report median + p95 for SLAs, not mean.
drills.solutionNotes: Heavy tails are normal in systems data — mean is often the wrong headline number.

drills.title: Construct a 95% confidence interval
drills.prompt: n = 100 coin flips, 55 heads. Assuming a normal approximation, construct a 95% CI for the true heads probability p. (Use z = 1.96.)
drills.solutionNotes: The interval width shrinks as 1/√n — quadruple the data to halve the error bar.

testCases.setup: function ci95(phat,n,z=1.96){const se=Math.sqrt(phat*(1-phat)/n);return [phat-z*se,phat+z*se].map(x=>+x.toFixed(3));}

drills.title: Interpret a p-value correctly
drills.prompt: An A/B test reports p = 0.03 for H₀: 'no difference in conversion'. The lift is 0.1% absolute (0.5% → 0.6%). Write two sentences: one correct interpretation of the p-value, one reason you might still not ship.
drills.expectedOutput: Correct: if there were truly no effect, we'd see data this extreme ~3% of the time. Might not ship: effect is tiny (practical insignificance) or the test was one of many without correction.
hints: p-value is about the null, not P(null is true).
drills.solutionNotes: Always pair p-values with effect size and business context.

testCases.setup: function shouldShip(p,alpha,absLift,minLift){return p<alpha && absLift>=minLift;}

drills.title: Fit a simple linear regression
drills.prompt: Points: (1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 5). Fit y = a + bx by least squares. Compute a, b, and the predicted ŷ at x = 4.
drills.solutionNotes: With only 3 points the fit is exact in spirit; with noise, residuals matter.

drills.title: Compute a 2D gradient
drills.prompt: f(x, y) = x² + 3xy + y². Compute ∇f at (1, 2). In which direction should you step to decrease f fastest, and what is the directional derivative in that direction?
drills.expectedOutput: ∇f = [2x+3y, 3x+2y] → [8, 7] at (1,2). Steepest descent: −∇f/||∇f||. Directional derivative along −∇f/||∇f|| is −||∇f|| = −√113.
hints: Partial w.r.t. x treats y as constant.
Steepest descent is opposite the gradient.
drills.solutionNotes: This is exactly what gradient descent does — step opposite ∇f with a learning rate.

testCases.setup: function grad(x,y){return [2*x+3*y,3*x+2*y];} function norm(v){return Math.sqrt(v[0]*v[0]+v[1]*v[1]);}

drills.title: Independence and inclusion-exclusion
drills.prompt: P(A)=0.4, P(B)=0.5, P(A∩B)=0.2. Are A and B independent? Compute P(A∪B). If they were mutually exclusive, what would P(A∪B) be instead?
drills.expectedOutput: P(A)P(B)=0.2=P(A∩B) → independent. P(A∪B)=0.4+0.5−0.2=0.7. Mutually exclusive would give 0.9 — conflating the two is a common bug.
drills.solutionNotes: Mutually exclusive implies P(A∩B)=0, which is much stronger than independence.

testCases.setup:
function indep(pA, pB, pAB) { return Math.abs(pA * pB - pAB) < 1e-9; }
function union(pA, pB, pAB) { return pA + pB - pAB; }

drills.title: Normal tail probability
drills.prompt: Latency is Normal(μ=100ms, σ=20ms). Approximate P(X > 140ms) using the z-score and a standard normal table (Φ(2) ≈ 0.977). What fraction is within 1σ of the mean?
hints: Tail probability is 1−Φ(z) for upper tail.
drills.solutionNotes: The 68–95–99.7 rule is the CLT's everyday face.

drills.title: Correlation pitfalls
drills.prompt: Dataset: ice cream sales and drowning deaths are strongly correlated (r≈0.9). (a) Does ice cream cause drowning? (b) Name the lurking variable. (c) When would correlation still be useful for engineers?
drills.expectedOutput: (a) No — correlation ≠ causation. (b) Summer/temperature drives both. (c) Feature screening, anomaly pairs, quick linear sanity checks — never causal claims.
hints: Look for a confounder that affects both series.
drills.solutionNotes: Always ask what third variable could explain both.

drills.title: Type I vs Type II errors
drills.prompt: A security patch test: H₀ = 'no change in error rate'. You fail to reject H₀, but errors actually rose 0.5%. (a) Which error type? (b) If α=0.05 and power=0.8, what does each number mean in one sentence?
drills.expectedOutput: (a) Type II error (false negative) — missed a real regression. α=5% false-alarm rate if H₀ true; power=80% chance to detect a real effect of the assumed size.
hints: Type I: reject true H₀. Type II: fail to reject false H₀.
Power = 1 − P(Type II) for a specific alternative.
drills.solutionNotes: Low power + 'not significant' ≠ 'no effect'.

testCases.setup: function errorType(h0True,rejected){if(h0True&&rejected)return "I";if(!h0True&&!rejected)return "II";return "ok";}

drills.title: Match distribution to scenario
drills.prompt: For each scenario, name the best distribution: (1) 10,000 coin flips, count heads. (2) Rare server crashes, ~2 per day. (3) Time until next request in a Poisson stream. (4) Average of 500 independent user latencies.
drills.expectedOutput: (1) Binomial (≈Normal for large n). (2) Poisson. (3) Exponential. (4) Normal for the sample mean (CLT).
hints: Waiting times → Exponential; rare counts in window → Poisson.
drills.solutionNotes: Pick the generative story, not just the shape of a histogram.

drills.title: CLT and required sample size
drills.prompt: A/B test on conversion rate: baseline 5%, MDE 1pp (to 6%). Rough rule: need ~16p(1−p)/δ² samples per arm for Normal approx (p=0.05, δ=0.01). Estimate n per arm. Why is n quadratic in 1/δ?
drills.expectedOutput: n ≈ 16×0.05×0.95/0.0001 ≈ 7,600 per arm. Halving MDE quadruples required n — detecting small lifts is expensive.
hints: Standard error of proportion shrinks as 1/√n.
Smaller δ → need more data to separate signal from noise.
drills.solutionNotes: This is why underpowered experiments waste weeks.

testCases.setup: function nPerArm(p,delta){return Math.ceil(16*p*(1-p)/(delta*delta));}

drills.title: Standard error vs standard deviation
drills.prompt: n=100 samples, sample mean x̄=50, sample SD s=10. Compute SE = s/√n and a 95% CI for the population mean (use z=1.96). Why is SE smaller than s?
drills.expectedOutput: SE=10/10=1; CI≈[48.04, 51.96]. SE describes uncertainty of the mean; s describes spread of individual points.
drills.solutionNotes: Report SE when talking about estimates; SD when talking about individuals.

testCases.setup: function seMean(s,n){return s/Math.sqrt(n);} function ci(xbar,se,z=1.96){return [xbar-z*se,xbar+z*se].map(v=>+v.toFixed(2));}

drills.title: Design an A/B test with power
drills.prompt: You want to test a new ranking model on search CTR (baseline 8%, hope for +0.5pp). Define: primary metric, MDE, α, target power, estimated n per arm, duration given 50k daily users split 50/50, peeking policy, and guardrail metrics.
drills.expectedOutput: A one-page test plan with n≈7k+ per arm (similar proportion formula), ~3–4 days runtime, no peeking until fixed n, guardrails on latency and null-result rate.
hints: One primary metric; everything else is diagnostic.
drills.solutionNotes: If you cannot power the MDE, extend duration or accept larger uncertainty.

drills.title: Interpret a sample ratio mismatch
drills.prompt: Planned 50/50 split; after 24h you observe 52% control / 48% treatment (n=100k total). χ² test flags SRM at p=0.001. What do you do before trusting the CTR lift?
drills.expectedOutput: Stop the test readout; investigate bucketing bugs, bot traffic, locale skew, or deployment issues. Do not ship on a broken randomization.
hints: SRM means the assignment mechanism is wrong.
Effect estimates are biased under SRM.
drills.solutionNotes: Spotify/Microsoft experimentation platforms treat SRM as a hard gate.

drills.title: Beta-Binomial posterior update
drills.prompt: Prior: Beta(1,1) (uniform) on conversion rate θ. Observe 8 successes in 100 trials. Compute the posterior Beta(9,93). Give the posterior mean and a 95% credible interval (approximate with Normal: mean ± 1.96√(pq/(n+2))).
drills.expectedOutput: Posterior mean = 9/102 ≈ 0.088; CI roughly [0.035, 0.141] — direct 'where θ probably lives' statement.
hints: Beta(a,b) + s successes, f failures → Beta(a+s, b+f).
drills.solutionNotes: Contrast with '95% of repeated CIs contain θ' — different question.

drills.title: MLE for Bernoulli trials
drills.prompt: n=100 Bernoulli trials, 55 successes. Write the likelihood L(p)=p^55(1−p)^45, take log, differentiate, and solve for p̂. Why is this the same as the sample proportion?
drills.expectedOutput: p̂=55/100=0.55. Log-likelihood derivative gives successes/n — MLE equals the intuitive estimator here.
hints: log L = 55 log p + 45 log(1−p) + const.
drills.solutionNotes: Cross-entropy minimization on {0,1} labels is the same story with more dimensions.

testCases.setup: function mleBernoulli(successes, trials) { return successes / trials; }

drills.title: Compute cross-entropy in bits
drills.prompt: True distribution P=(0.5, 0.5); model Q=(0.9, 0.1). Compute H(P) and cross-entropy H(P,Q) in bits (log base 2). Which term dominates?
drills.expectedOutput: H(P)=1 bit. H(P,Q)=−0.5 log₂0.9−0.5 log₂0.1≈0.47+1.66≈2.13 bits. The Q=0.1 on the true class (prob 0.5) dominates — being confidently wrong is expensive.
hints: Cross-entropy uses Q as the model distribution.
drills.solutionNotes: This is why softmax+cross-entropy punishes confident wrong answers harshly.

testCases.setup:
function entropy(P) { return -P.reduce((s, p) => s + (p > 0 ? p * Math.log2(p) : 0), 0); }
function crossEntropy(P, Q) { return -P.reduce((s, p, i) => s + (p > 0 ? p * Math.log2(Q[i]) : 0), 0); }
testCases.run:
const P = [0.5, 0.5], Q = [0.9, 0.1];
console.log(entropy(P).toFixed(2), crossEntropy(P, Q).toFixed(2));

drills.title: Compute covariance and correlation matrix
drills.prompt: Given 3 aligned series (n=20 each), compute the 3×3 sample covariance matrix and correlation matrix by hand or in code (loops only). Verify diagonal covariances equal variances and corr diagonal = 1.
drills.expectedOutput: Symmetric matrices; corr[i,j] = cov[i,j]/(σ_i σ_j).
hints: Center each series first for covariance.
drills.solutionNotes: On returns, not prices — state which you used.

drills.title: Diagnose overfit with a train/holdout split
drills.prompt: Fit a degree-1 vs degree-5 polynomial to 30 noisy points (50/50 split). Report train vs holdout MSE for both. Which overfits and how do you know?
drills.expectedOutput: Degree-5 lower train MSE, worse holdout MSE — classic overfit signature.
hints: Same random split for both models.
MSE on holdout is the honest score.
drills.solutionNotes: This is the adult version of 'great backtest, no holdout'.

drills.title: Identify rank and column space
drills.prompt: A = [[1,2,3],[2,4,6],[1,1,0]]. What is rank(A)? Describe the column space in one sentence. Can you solve Ax=b for b=(3,6,2)?
drills.expectedOutput: rank=2 (row2=2×row1). Column space is span{(1,2,1),(2,4,1)}. Ax=b consistent — b is in column space.
hints: b must be a linear combo of columns.
drills.solutionNotes: Rank-deficient X in OLS needs regularization or constraint.

drills.title: PCA variance explained
drills.prompt: 2D data with cov matrix [[4,3],[3,9]]. Eigenvalues are 10 and 3. What fraction of variance do the top 1 and top 2 components explain? Why center before PCA?
drills.expectedOutput: PC1: 10/13≈77%; both: 100%. Centering removes the mean offset so variance is spread, not dominated by the origin.
drills.solutionNotes: Connect to impl-pca-from-scratch artifact.

drills.title: Log returns and annualized volatility
drills.prompt: From 5 consecutive adjusted closes, compute log returns r_t = ln(P_t/P_{t-1}). Estimate daily σ and annualize with √252. Show one line of code or formula per step.
drills.expectedOutput: 4 log returns; σ_daily; σ_annual = σ_daily×√252.
hints: Align returns to t, not t-1 price.
Use sample std dev on returns.
drills.solutionNotes: Feed quant-returns-pipeline artifact with real data next.

drills.title: Interpret an ACF plot
drills.prompt: Daily returns show ACF lag-1 ≈ 0.02, all within confidence band. Prices show ACF lag-1 ≈ 0.98. Interpret each — which series is closer to a random walk in returns?
drills.expectedOutput: Return ACF ≈0 → little linear memory (efficient-market-ish). Price ACF high → non-stationary levels; difference of prices (returns) matters.
hints: ACF on levels confounds trend with memory.
Returns stationarity is the usual assumption.
drills.solutionNotes: Momentum strategies need structure in returns, not just trending charts.

drills.title: Simulate a two-state Markov chain
drills.prompt: Transition matrix P=[[0.9,0.1],[0.2,0.8]] on states {0,1}. Simulate 1000 steps from state 0. Estimate stationary proportion of time in state 1.
drills.expectedOutput: ≈1/3 time in state 1 (solve πP=π → π=(2/3,1/3)). Simulation should be close.
hints: Each step: sample next state from P[current].
drills.solutionNotes: Random walk is a special Markov chain on ℤ.

drills.title: Sharpe ratio and max drawdown
drills.prompt: Given 252 daily returns with mean 0.04%/day and σ=1%/day (rf=0), compute annualized Sharpe. Equity curve peaks at 1.20 then troughs at 0.95 — max drawdown?
drills.solutionNotes: Report both return and pain — humans feel drawdown.

testCases.setup: function annSharpe(mean,sigma,rf=0){return (mean*252-rf)/(sigma*Math.sqrt(252));} function maxDD(peak,trough){return (trough-peak)/peak;}

drills.title: Design an honest momentum backtest
drills.prompt: Outline a 12-month momentum rule on 3 ETFs: train/holdout split, benchmark (SPY), transaction cost assumption, metrics (CAGR, Sharpe, max DD), and three ways your backtest could lie to you.
drills.expectedOutput: Written protocol before code; explicit holdout; limitations section mandatory.
hints: Survivorship and snooping are top lies.
drills.solutionNotes: If you cannot write limitations first, you are curve-fitting.

drills.title: Dot product and matmul shapes
drills.prompt: Vectors a=[1,2,3], b=[4,5,6]. Compute dot(a,b). Matrices A (2×3) and B (3×2): is A·B valid? What is the output shape?
drills.expectedOutput: dot=32; A·B is 2×2.
hints: Inner dimensions must match for matmul.
drills.solutionNotes: Shape errors are the #1 silent bug in ML code.

testCases.setup: function dot(a,b){return a.reduce((s,x,i)=>s+x*b[i],0);} function matmulShape(a,b){return [a.length,b[0].length];}

drills.title: One SGD step on a quadratic
drills.prompt: Loss L(w)=(w−3)². At w=0 with lr=0.1, compute gradient and one gradient-descent update.
drills.solutionNotes: Quadratic is the sanity check for any optimizer.

testCases.setup: function step(w,lr){const g=2*(w-3);return w-lr*g;}

drills.title: Chain rule on a tiny graph
drills.prompt: y = (2x+1)³. Compute dy/dx at x=1 via chain rule (u=2x+1, y=u³).
drills.solutionNotes: Backprop is automated chain rule on the compute graph.

testCases.setup: function dydx(x){const u=2*x+1;return 3*u*u*2;}

drills.title: Softmax and cross-entropy loss
drills.prompt: Logits [2,1,0.1], true class index 0. Compute softmax probabilities and −log p(true).
hints: Subtract max logit before exp for stability.
drills.solutionNotes: Log-sum-exp trick prevents overflow.

testCases.setup:
function softmax(xs){const m=Math.max(...xs);const ex=xs.map(x=>Math.exp(x-m));const s=ex.reduce((a,b)=>a+b,0);return ex.map(e=>e/s);}
function xent(logits,y){const p=softmax(logits);return -Math.log(p[y]);}

drills.title: AdamW vs L2-on-gradient
drills.prompt: Weight w=2, grad=0.5, lr=0.1, decay=0.01. Compare one step: (a) L2 in gradient w−lr·(grad+decay·w) vs (b) AdamW-style w−lr·grad then w·(1−lr·decay).
drills.expectedOutput: L2→1.33; decoupled→1.33 then shrink (slightly different effective decay).
hints: Coupled L2 adds decay·w to the gradient.
AdamW applies decay directly on weights.
drills.solutionNotes: Decoupled decay is the default in modern LM training.

testCases.setup: function l2Step(w,g,lr,d){return w-lr*(g+d*w);} function adamwStep(w,g,lr,d){return (w-lr*g)*(1-lr*d);}

drills.title: One BPE merge step
drills.prompt: Corpus tokenized as [l,o,w,e,r,l,o,w,e,s,t]. Count adjacent pairs; merge the most frequent pair once. What is the new sequence?
drills.expectedOutput: lo appears twice → merge lo → [lo,w,e,r,lo,w,e,s,t].
hints: Merge highest-count pair into one token.
drills.solutionNotes: BPE grows vocab by merging, not splitting.

drills.title: Perplexity from average NLL
drills.prompt: Average negative log-likelihood per token = 2.3 nats. Compute perplexity = exp(NLL).
hints: Perplexity is exp of average NLL.
drills.solutionNotes: PPX 10 ≈ uniform over ~10 tokens on average.

testCases.setup: function perplexity(avgNll){return Math.exp(avgNll);}

drills.title: Temperature scaling logits
drills.prompt: Logits [2,1,0]. Apply temperature T=2 and T=0.5 before softmax. Which T makes the distribution sharper?
drills.expectedOutput: T<1 sharpens (more greedy); T>1 flattens.
hints: Divide logits by T before softmax.
drills.solutionNotes: Temperature trades diversity vs determinism.

testCases.setup:
function softmax(xs){const m=Math.max(...xs);const ex=xs.map(x=>Math.exp(x-m));const s=ex.reduce((a,b)=>a+b,0);return ex.map(e=>e/s);}
function scaled(T,xs){return softmax(xs.map(x=>x/T));}

drills.title: Sinusoidal position encoding
drills.prompt: pos=1, i=0, d=4: PE(pos,2i)=sin(pos/10000^(2i/d)). Compute the first two dims for pos=1.
drills.expectedOutput: sin(1), cos(1) pattern for even/odd dims.
hints: Even dims sin, odd dims cos in original paper.
drills.solutionNotes: Positions are added to token embeddings before attention.

testCases.setup: function pe(pos,i,d){return Math.sin(pos/Math.pow(10000,2*i/d));}

drills.title: Scaled dot-product attention weights
drills.prompt: Q=K=[[1,0],[0,1]], V=[[1,2],[3,4]], d_k=2. Compute softmax(QKᵀ/√d_k)·V for one query row.
drills.expectedOutput: Uniform weights 0.5/0.5 → output [2,3].
drills.solutionNotes: Scaling stops dot products from saturating softmax.

testCases.setup: function attnRow(q,k,v,dk){const scores=k.map(ki=>q.reduce((s,qj,j)=>s+qj*ki[j],0)/Math.sqrt(dk));const m=Math.max(...scores);const ex=scores.map(s=>Math.exp(s-m));const w=ex.map(e=>e/ex.reduce((a,b)=>a+b,0));return w.map((wi,i)=>wi*v[i].reduce((s,vj,j)=>s+wj*vj,0));}

drills.title: Split embedding across heads
drills.prompt: d_model=512, h=8. What is head_dim? If you project Q to 512 dims total, how many dims per head?
hints: Each head runs attention in its own subspace.
drills.solutionNotes: Multi-head = parallel attention at smaller width.

testCases.setup: function headDim(d,h){return d/h;}

drills.title: Pre-LayerNorm residual block
drills.prompt: x=[1,2], sublayer(x)=x+[0.1,−0.1]. Write pre-norm update: x + sublayer(LayerNorm(x)). If LayerNorm is identity here, new x?
drills.expectedOutput: [1.1,1.9] — residual preserves a gradient highway.
drills.solutionNotes: Pre-LN trains deeper stacks more reliably than post-LN.

testCases.setup: function preNorm(x,delta){return x.map((xi,i)=>xi+delta[i]);}

drills.title: Overfit a single batch sanity check
drills.prompt: Training loss stuck high on 1 batch of 8 examples. Name two data bugs to check before changing the architecture.
drills.expectedOutput: Wrong labels/shapes, train-eval leakage, broken tokenizer, masked tokens all padding, etc.
hints: Karpathy recipe: overfit one batch first.
If you cannot overfit one batch, data or loss is wrong.
drills.solutionNotes: Architecture debugging comes after the dataloader is trusted.

drills.title: Checkpoint must include optimizer state
drills.prompt: You save weights only (no optimizer state) at step 10k, resume training. What goes wrong with Adam momentum estimates?
drills.expectedOutput: Optimizer moments reset — loss spike / different trajectory; effective warmup replay.
hints: Adam stores m and v per parameter.
Resume needs weights + optimizer + step + RNG if exact replay.
drills.solutionNotes: Production checkpoints are more than model.state_dict().

testCases.setup:
function checkpoint(state) { return JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(state)); }
function resume(ckpt) { return { ...ckpt, step: ckpt.step, weights: [...ckpt.weights] }; }

drills.title: LoRA parameter count
drills.prompt: W is 4096×4096 (frozen). LoRA rank r=8. How many trainable params in A (4096×r) and B (r×4096)?
drills.expectedOutput: 4096·8 + 8·4096 = 65,536 trainable vs 16M full matrix.
hints: Train A and B only; W stays fixed.
drills.solutionNotes: LoRA trades expressivity for a tiny trainable footprint.

testCases.setup: function loraParams(d,r){return 2*d*r;}

drills.title: Estimate training token budget

drills.title: Dijkstra shortest path
drills.prompt: Weighted graph: 0—1—2 with weights 1 and 4, and 0—2 weight 10. Shortest 0→2?
drills.expectedOutput: Cost 5 via node 1.
drills.solutionNotes: Non-negative weights required.

testCases.setup: function dijkstra(g,s,t){const dist=new Map([[s,0]]);const pq=[[0,s]];while(pq.length){pq.sort((a,b)=>a[0]-b[0]);const[d,u]=pq.shift();if(u===t)return d;if(d>dist.get(u))continue;for(const[e,w]of g[u]||[]){const nd=d+w;if(nd<(dist.get(e)??Infinity)){dist.set(e,nd);pq.push([nd,e]);}}}return -1;}

drills.expectedOutput: 2 components.
drills.solutionNotes: Union-find is O(α(n)) amortized per op.

testCases.setup: function countComp(n,edges){const p=Array.from({length:n},(_,i)=>i);function find(x){return p[x]===x?x:(p[x]=find(p[x]));}function uni(a,b){a=find(a);b=find(b);if(a!==b)p[b]=a;}edges.forEach(([a,b])=>uni(a,b));return new Set(p.map(find)).size;}

drills.title: Minimum path sum in a grid
drills.prompt: Grid [[1,3,1],[1,5,1],[4,2,1]]. Min path sum top-left to bottom-right moving only right/down?
drills.solutionNotes: 2D DP pattern: carry best-so-far into each cell.

testCases.setup: function minPath(g){const r=g.length,c=g[0].length;const dp=Array.from({length:r},()=>Array(c).fill(0));dp[0][0]=g[0][0];for(let i=1;i<c;i++)dp[0][i]=dp[0][i-1]+g[0][i];for(let i=1;i<r;i++)dp[i][0]=dp[i-1][0]+g[i][0];for(let i=1;i<r;i++)for(let j=1;j<c;j++)dp[i][j]=g[i][j]+Math.min(dp[i-1][j],dp[i][j-1]);return dp[r-1][c-1];}

drills.title: Merge overlapping intervals

testCases.setup: function merge(iv){if(!iv.length)return[];iv.sort((a,b)=>a[0]-b[0]);const out=[iv[0]];for(let i=1;i<iv.length;i++){const last=out[out.length-1];if(iv[i][0]<=last[1])last[1]=Math.max(last[1],iv[i][1]);else out.push(iv[i]);}return out;}
